claims.text: Time travel to the future is scientifically demonstrated by the laws of physics (relativistic time dilation), but time travel to the past remains uncertain — circumstantial evidence of past visits exists but every classic 'time traveler' story examined in this video turns out to be a hoax, urban legend, or known artifact misidentified.

1970s, maybe even earlier.
I first heard it from my dad when I was a kid, but it's not true.
It's actually an excerpt from a short story called I'm Scared, which was written by Jack
Finney, you might know, is the author of The Body Snatchers.
The story was published in Collier's magazine in 1951, about the time when Rudolfensis
supposedly appeared.
Nice to believe it. I know you did.
The Andrew Carlson story is one of my favorites.
I love this one. It has all the makings of a great conspiracy, millions of dollars, a
mysterious benefactor, a missing person.
But this one originated as a fictional piece in Weekly World News, a satirical newspaper.
Later, the story was picked up by Yahoo!
News. So naturally, people believe the story.
It was then reported by other newspapers and magazines as fact.
And this drove word of mouth, creating one of the great modern urban legends.
So the lesson here, we don't believe anything the media says you're learning.
Ok, so the Mike Tyson fight, although

claims.rationale: Special-relativity time dilation is empirically confirmed (Hafele-Keating experiment with atomic clocks on jets, GPS satellite corrections). Past-directed time travel via closed timelike curves is mathematically permitted by GR but unproven physically. The host's framing here sets up the debunking section that follows.

claims.text: The Rudolph Fentz story — a man in 1876 clothes hit by a taxi in 1950 Times Square whose 1876 missing-person record allegedly closed the case 75 years later — is a fictional excerpt from Jack Finney's 1951 short story 'I'm Scared' published in Collier's magazine, not a real police case.

the signs like he'd never seen them before.
He then tried to cross the street against the traffic lights, was hit by a speeding taxi
and died.
Pockets of his clothing had coins and currency that had not been in circulation for
decades.
Yet many of them were in mint condition.

from a livery stable, a brass slug from a saloon and a letter postmarked
1876. He also had identification bearing the name Rudolph Fense with an address on Fifth

residence. And no one there had ever heard of Rudolph fense.

claims.rationale: Jack Finney's 'I'm Scared' (Collier's, September 1951) is well-documented as the origin of the Rudolph Fentz story. The transformation from short story into widely-believed 'true' anecdote (often attributed to Akron Beacon Journal or other outlets in retellings) is a textbook urban-legend evolution. Finney did write 'The Body Snatchers' (1955).

News.

claims.rationale: The 'Andrew Carlssin' (originally) time-traveling stockbroker story was indeed a 2003 Weekly World News piece and was widely re-reported by aggregators including Yahoo News despite the source being explicitly satirical. There is no real SEC case file, no actual Carlson, and the FBI never arrested him.

claims.rationale: The Tyson-McNeeley footage circulates online as a 'time traveler smartphone' clip. The Casio QV-10 (1995) was indeed an early consumer digital still camera with LCD viewfinder, and could plausibly explain a small handheld device in 1995. The host hedges with 'probably maybe' which is appropriate given the ambiguous footage.

claims.rationale: The 'Time Traveler at South Fork Bridge' photo (1941, Gold Bridge BC) is real and the museum's authenticity claim is on record. The Montreal Maroons branded sweater (the team folded in 1938) explanation is well-known; wraparound side-shield welder/skier glasses existed; small folding cameras like the Kodak Folding Pocket had been mainstream for decades. So mundane explanation holds.

claims.rationale: The Chaplin clip 'time traveler' was debunked years ago — the Siemens 1924 carbon-amplified hearing aid (or similar) is the leading explanation. The DuPont 1938 footage explanation is harder: the family's 'experimental wireless phone' claim is real but the size/battery physics objection the host raises is sound (1930s portable radios were huge). The host correctly leaves DuPont as 'I don't know if this one is debunked.'

claims.text: The two 'physical artifact' time-travel claims — a Swiss-engraved watch ring found in a 400-year-old Ming Dynasty tomb in Shaanxi (2008) and an Apollo-suited astronaut carved into the 12th-century Cathedral of Salamanca — are both definitively debunked: the watch is a hoax (Photoshopped/planted) and the astronaut is a 1992 restoration addition by the artist.

claims.rationale: The 2008 Ming Dynasty 'Swiss watch ring' was widely covered then largely abandoned as photoshop/hoax — no peer-reviewed archaeology supports it. The Salamanca astronaut (Hieronimus Bosch-style figure) is real and was added by sculptor Miguel Romero during the 1992 restoration of the Puerta de Ramos — both are settled debunkings with documentary evidence.

claims.text: Why time-travel stories captivate us is itself the deeper truth: we are obsessed with these stories because we are obsessed with time itself — time is finite, irreplaceable, and (contra the cliché) more valuable than money since money can be made and remade but lost time cannot.

evidence.quote:
years from now, when someone looks at that church, they are
going to be confused.
They say time is the fire in which we burn.
So why do we want so badly for these stories to be true, why are we so obsessed with time
travel?
Well, probably because we're obsessed with time.
Time rules our lives.
Time creates possibilities.
The time also ends possibilities.
It's finite. The expression time is money isn't exactly true.
Time is much more valuable than money.
Wealth can be made and lost and made again.
But time once we lose it, we can never get it back.
Time is the most valuable thing you have.
Spend it wisely. Thanks for hanging out with us today.
My name is A.J. That Tigerfish, this has been the Y files.
If you had fun or learned anything today.
Help us out by liking, commenting or sharing this video.
Only with your help can we defeat the algorithm.
Let's crush the algorithm until this time.
Be safe. Be kind and know that you are appreciated.

claims.rationale: This is the host's closing thesis — explicitly value-laden moral commentary about time vs. money. The descriptive part (most time-travel stories don't survive scrutiny) follows from the previous claims. The 'time more valuable than money' framing is opinion. directTruth omitted because it's a values claim, not a verifiable fact.
